title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Miss Marple suggests to Gwendolyn that her terrifying visions are not supernatural occurrences, but rather repressed childhood memories. Miss Marple observes that Gwendolyn has confused two different journeys and notes that Gwendolyn’s intimate familiarity with the layout, wallpaper patterns, and mahogany bathtub of her current house matches a childhood home in England. Furthermore, Miss Marple posits that Gwendolyn’s memory of seeing a woman named Helen being strangled is a suppressed trauma from her childhood, noting that Gwendolyn's description of looking through gaps in railings rather than over them is characteristic of a child's behavior.

To investigate this theory, Miss Marple advises Gwendolyn to contact her Aunt Alison to confirm if she ever lived in England. Ten days later, Miss Marple meets Gwendolyn and her husband, Jules Reed, at an inn in Mayfair. Gwendolyn presents an airmail letter from Aunt Alison (Mrs. Danby) that confirms Miss Marple's hypothesis. The letter reveals that Gwendolyn’s mother, Margaret, died in India after marrying Colonel Hardy. Following Margaret's death, the Colonel intended to bring Gwendolyn to England, but he instead married a young woman and later separated from her. Eventually, the Colonel sent Gwendolyn to live with relatives in England via a British nursemaid. The letter also notes that the Colonel passed away in a nursing home approximately one year after Gwendolyn arrived in England.
